ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Vietnamese corn dessert soup (Chè Bắp). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic sweet soup food product class. A more specific Vietnamese dessert soup class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (sweet soups and porridges) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for Chè Bắp. No dedicated Vietnamese corn dessert soup code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Vietnamese corn dessert soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chè Bắp is approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve dessert portion as commonly sold at Vietnamese dessert stalls and food delivery platforms in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sweet corn, yellow, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt; Coconut milk, canned; Glutinous rice, cooked; Sugar, granulated — composite estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore entry exists for Chè Bắp as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for sweet corn (cooked), coconut milk (canned), glutinous rice (cooked), and granulated sugar, weighted to reflect a typical Vietnamese Chè Bắp recipe (approximately 55% sweet corn, 20% coconut milk, 15% glutinous rice, 10% sugar and water). The input record values (energy 128 kcal, protein 1.8 g, fat 4.8 g, carbohydrate 20.0 g, sugar 11.2 g, sodium 12 mg, dietary fibre 1.2 g, saturated fat 3.6 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained. Cholesterol is confirmed 0 mg as all ingredients are plant-based. Trans fat is confirmed 0 g by formulation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for glutinous rice-based sweet soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chè Bắp (Vietnamese corn dessert soup) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 75 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: glutinous rice (GI ~98, University of Sydney GI Database) and sweet corn (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by their proportional contribution to total carbohydrate (~40% glutinous rice, ~35% corn, ~25% added sugar GI ~65). Weighted composite GI ≈ (0.40×98) + (0.35×52) + (0.25×65) = 39.2 + 18.2 + 16.25 ≈ 73.7, rounded to 75. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (75 × 50.0) / 100 = 37.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.2 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium (12 mg/100 g); saturated fat from coconut milk (3.6 g/100 g) contributes to vascular stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g, reduce added sugar, and substitute full-fat coconut milk with light coconut milk to lower both glycaemic and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice (GI ~98) and added sugar (11.2 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) produce a high composite GI (~75) and a very high glycaemic load per serving (~37.5), causing rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Chè Bắp; if consumed, replace glutinous rice with regular corn, reduce added sugar substantially, and limit serving to no more than 100 g to reduce glycaemic load to a manageable level.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk at 3.6 g/100 g (9.0 g per 250 g serving) contributes to LDL cholesterol elevation; high sugar content (11.2 g/100 g) promotes triglyceride synthesis and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Chè Bắp to occasional small servings (≤150 g), use light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat, and minimise added sugar to lower triglyceride and LDL risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (3.6 g/100 g from coconut milk) and high sugar (11.2 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory mediators; glutinous rice is a refined carbohydrate that elevates inflammatory glycaemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should reduce added sugar, substitute full-fat coconut milk with light coconut milk or unsweetened almond milk, and replace glutinous rice with lower-GI whole grains to reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chè Bắp (Corn Dessert Soup) is a traditional Vietnamese sweet dessert soup made from sweet corn kernels, glutinous rice, coconut milk, sugar, and pandan leaves, simmered together until the corn is tender and the broth is lightly thickened and fragrant. The dish is served warm and has a mildly sweet, creamy flavour with a soft, slightly chewy texture from the glutinous rice and tender corn kernels. It is a popular street food and home dessert throughout Vietnam and is widely available at Vietnamese dessert stalls in Singapore. Nutritionally, Chè Bắp provides approximately 128 kcal per 100 g, with 20 g carbohydrate, 4.8 g fat (predominantly saturated fat at 3.6 g from coconut milk), 1.8 g protein, and 11.2 g sugar. Dietary fibre is low at 1.2 g/100 g. The dish has a high composite glycaemic index (~75) driven by glutinous rice (GI ~98) and added sugar, making it clinically significant for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. A standard 250 g bowl delivers approximately 320 kcal and a glycaemic load of ~37.5, which is very high. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is suitable for halal diets. Coconut milk is a tree nut allergen and should be noted for allergy-sensitive individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of Chè Bắp?
Is Chè Bắp suitable for diabetics?
What is Vietnamese sweet corn soup?
What is the glycaemic index of Chè Bắp?

Can I eat Chè Bắp if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central) contains a direct entry for Chè Bắp as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for sweet corn, coconut milk, glutinous rice, and sugar, weighted by typical recipe proportions. The input values are consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese dessert soup is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as a single dish entry as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero by formulation as all ingredients (sweet corn, glutinous rice, coconut milk, sugar, pandan) are plant-based and contain no industrial trans fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore; corrected to Vietnam as Chè Bắp is a traditional Vietnamese dish; Singapore is the serving market context. GI estimated at 75 (high) as a weighted composite of glutinous rice (GI ~98), sweet corn (GI ~52), and added sugar (GI ~65) per University of Sydney GI Database analogous values.
